title: Image Handling
text: Image Handling Uniface can display and print pictures that are stored as image files on disk, as glyphs, or BLOBs in a database. The type of picture can affect performance. Table 1. Referencing Pictures Notation Meaning Performance Impact @ FileName Load the image from the disk file FileName . Loading an image from disk is far more expensive in terms of I/O than loading the equivalent glyph. This is not an important consideration if you do not need to do this very often, or you do it only temporarily in the Uniface IDE. # FieldName Load the image from the BLOB field FieldName . Depending on your DBMS, loading a BLOB from the database is probably faster than loading a disk file. & URL In server pages only, display the image specified by the URL. In web applications, this is the fastest way to load images. Uniface is not actually involved in displaying the image, and depending on the environment, images may be loaded from cache. ^ Glyph Load the glyph named Glyph . Glyphs are Uniface-proprietary compiled images that may be used in desktop applications. The location of glyphs depends on the configuration, but may be in a UAR file or the Repository. Fields of any data type can be picture fields: Fields with data type Image—images, glyphs, or BLOBs are loaded automatically Fields with data type String—images and glyphs (but not BLOBs) are loaded automatically if the data uses the picture notation. If the string picture field does not conform to this syntax, a ValRep list must be defined. Fields with other data types—require a ValRep list to load an image or glyph because Uniface needs to know how the data in the field should be interpreted. If no path is specified for an image file ( @ FileName ), Uniface looks for the image in the current directory or in the directory specified by the Images assignment setting. If you use an explicit path to specify an image, this setting is ignored. Related Links Glyphs Glyphs are images in a Uniface-proprietary format, which can be used as background images in forms, entities and application shells, and as icons in widgets, panel buttons, menus, and drag-and-drop formats.
